frameworks.name: signal detection theory
frameworks.one_line: classical SDT extended with information-theoretic approximations to proportion correct

frameworks.name: information theory
frameworks.one_line: entropy-based decomposition bounds classification performance in stimulus discrimination

core_question: Can information-theoretic quantities provide a tractable lower bound on the probability of correct classification in signal detection?
key_assumptions: stimuli are drawn from generating distributions to be classified
classification performance can be characterized via probability of correct response
observers have an internal model of class distributions that may differ from truth
manipulations: number of stimulus categories beyond binary
discrepancy between observer model and true generating distributions
key_findings: a lower bound on proportion correct can be expressed via three information-theoretic factors
the bound decomposes into prior uncertainty, class separability, and observer-model discrepancy
the bound generalizes to multiple dimensions and many stimulus categories where integration is hard
the bound does not require Gaussian distributional assumptions
interpretations: information theory provides a tractable substitute for direct integration in high-dimensional SDT
classification accuracy can be understood as jointly limited by prior, separability, and model mismatch
limitations_noted: the bound is loose compared to direct integration of proportion correct
